Council(a)Decides that the thirty-second session of the Committee of Experts on International Cooperation in Tax Matters will be held in New York from 23 to 26 March 2026;(b)Approves the provisional agenda of the thirty-second session of the Committee, as set out below:Provisional agenda of the thirty-second session of the Committee of Experts on International Cooperation in Tax Matters1.Opening of the session by the Co-Chairs.2.Adoption of the agenda and organization of work.3.Discussion of issues related to international cooperation in tax matters:(a)Issues related to the United Nations Model Tax Convention;(b)Update of the United Nations Manual for the Negotiation of Bilateral Tax Treaties between Developed and Developing Countries;(c)Transfer pricing;(d)Taxation issues related to the digitalized and globalized economy; (e)Tax administration and artificial intelligence;(f)Taxation of the extractive industries;(g)Indirect taxes; (h)Dispute avoidance and resolution;(i)Environmental taxation;(j)Wealth taxation;(k)Tax and gender;(l)Capacity-building;(m)Other matters for consideration.4.Provisional agenda of the thirty-third session of the Committee.5.Arrangements for adopting the report of the Committee on its thirty-second session.Chapter II Introduction2.Pursuant to Economic and Social Council resolutions 2004/69, 2017/2 and 2025/317, the thirty-first session of the Committee of Experts on International Cooperation in Tax Matters was held in Geneva from 21 to 24 October 2025. The in-person session was attended by 23 members of the Committee, and by 173 registered in-person observers. The session was also broadcast on UN Web TV. 3.The present report serves to summarize Committee discussions and decisions taken on the items set out in the provisional agenda of the Committee at its thirty-first session, as adopted by the Committee (E/C.18/2025/4) as follows:Provisional agenda1.Opening of the session by the representative of the Secretary-General. 2.Election of the Chair or Co-Chairs and Vice-Chairs of the Committee. 3.Remarks by the Chair or Co-Chairs of the Committee. 4.Adoption of the agenda. 5.Discussion of issues related to international cooperation in tax matters: (a)Taxation and the Sustainable Development Goals;(b)Issues related to the United Nations Model Tax Convention;(c)Review and possible update of the United Nations Manual for the Negotiation of Bilateral Tax Treaties between Developed and Developing Countries; (d)Procedural issues for the Committee; (e)Other areas for Committee consideration and guidance as part of its work programme.6.Provisional agenda of the thirty-second session. 7.Arrangements for adopting the report of the Committee on its thirty-first session.Chapter III Organization of the sessionAgenda item 1 Opening of the thirty-first session by the representative of the Secretary-General4.The Director of the Financing for Sustainable Development Office, Shari Spiegel, opened the session on behalf of the Secretary-General in a closed meeting. She then presided over the election of the Co-Chairs of the Committee.Agenda item 2 Election of the Chair or Co-Chairs and Vice-Chairs of the Committee5.At a closed meeting, Trude Steinnes Sønvisen and Mathew Olusanya Gbonjubola were elected by acclamation as Co-Chairs. Yan Xiong, Enrique Bolado Muñoz, Kapembwa Namuyemba-Sikombe and Angie Clocheret were elected by acclamation as Vice-Chairs, completing the Bureau of the Committee. The outcomes of the elections were announced in the open part of the session.Agenda item 3 Remarks by the Chair or Co-Chairs of the Committee6.The Co-Chair, Mr. Gbonjubola, spoke at the first open meeting of the session, welcoming members and observers to the first meeting of the 2025–2029 membership. He expressed his gratitude for the opportunity to serve on the Committee, including as Co-Chair, and confidence in the newly elected Bureau, which would assist the Committee in meeting its mandate. He was followed by the other Co-Chair, Ms. Steinnes Sønvisen, who welcomed participants from diverse professional and geographical backgrounds, emphasizing collaboration, inclusivity and the importance of stakeholders’ contributions to advancing the Committee’s objectives during the new term.7.The Director of the Financing for Sustainable Development Office, representing the Secretary-General, congratulated the new Co-Chairs and Committee members, highlighting both their expertise and the significance of the 2025–2029 term. She reflected on the accomplishments of the 2021–2025 membership and emphasized continuity in fostering inclusive, practical and development-oriented international tax cooperation. Linking the work of the Committee to the financing for development process and the Sevilla Commitment, she underscored its role in strengthening domestic resource mobilization, supporting developing countries and contributing to the role of the United Nations in enhancing and making international tax cooperation more inclusive and effective. She extended her best wishes for a productive session and a successful four-year term.8.The Under-Secretary-General for Economic and Social Affairs, Li Junhua, also addressed the participants, conveying the importance of the work of the Committee to collective sustainable development efforts, in particular in implementing the Sevilla Commitment across its action areas and finalizing preparations for the Second World Summit for Social Development, held in Doha from 4 to 6 November 2025. He indicated the focus of the Summit on inequality and rebuilding social contracts – areas where progressive tax systems are fundamental. Mr. Li noted that the regular budget resources for the Committee’s secretariat would likely become even more constrained, making continued and expanded partnership support essential. He thanked current contributors and welcomed additional contributions to ensure that the Committee’s vital work continued effectively.Agenda item 4 Adoption of the agenda9.The provisional agenda for the thirty-first session (E/C.18/2025/4) was adopted without objection. Chapter IVIssues related to international cooperation in tax mattersAgenda item 5: Discussion of issues related to international cooperation in tax mattersA.Taxation and the Sustainable Development Goals10.The Co-Chair, Mr. Gbonjubola, remarked that it was increasingly evident that the work of the Committee contributed to advancing the Sustainable Development Goals. He encouraged members to keep that overarching objective in mind as the Committee continued its work across various streams. He then opened the floor for comments on Goal-related issues that had not yet been addressed during the session. One member welcomed the growing emphasis by the Committee on Goal 5 (“Achieve gender equality and empower all women and girls”) and stressed the importance of its integration into the work of the Committee.B.Issues related to the United Nations Model Double Taxation Convention between Developed and Developing Countries11.Irving Ojeda Alvarez of the Secretariat presented a conference room paper on the possible updating of the United Nations Model Double Taxation Convention between Developed and Developing Countries (United Nations Model Tax Convention) during the Committee’s 2025–2029 term (E/C.18/2025/CRP.22). She explained that the Committee mandate was to keep the Model Convention under review and update it as necessary. 12.Ms. Ojeda Alvarez noted that the previous Committee membership had completed a substantial revision of the Model Convention, introducing several new and revised articles, including: article 5A (Income from the exploration for or exploitation of natural resources); article 12AA (Income from services, replacing articles 12A and 14); and article 12C (Insurance premiums); the inclusion of the “subject-to-tax rule” in article 1 (3); and revisions to the definition of royalties and updates to related commentaries. She clarified that it was not necessary for each membership of the Committee to complete a full revision of the Model Convention, although it had been updated under recent memberships. While the 2025 update represented substantial progress, several technical matters identified by the previous Committee, and through stakeholder submissions, remained outstanding. Those issues were included in the conference room paper and categorized in three groups, for discussion by the Committee.13.The first group comprised items based on previous Committee recommendations and related inputs, namely: (a) the taxation of permanent establishments, including clarification of the “force of attraction” rule and profit attribution for services permanent establishments; (b) source rules based on the place of payment, in particular for passive income articles; (c) the definition of immovable property, including the treatment of mineral royalties; (d) consequential changes to reflect the subject-to-tax rule; and (e) the scope of article 21 (Other income), in particular paragraph 3.14.The second group addressed broader structural issues, in particular a review and possible modernization of the “permanent establishment” concept, including the consideration of non-physical or digital permanent establishments, the concept of significant economic presence and nexus thresholds based on revenue or use.15.The third group encompassed additional technical topics, including: (a) alignment between article 22 (Capital) and article 13 (Capital gains) and the potential expansion of royalties to cover all forms of copyright; (b) review of definitions under article 10 (Dividends) and article 6 (Income from immovable property); and (c) evaluation of secondary source rules for passive income articles.16.Members expressed broad support for establishing a subcommittee on the United Nations Model Tax Convention. Most speakers emphasized that this workstream on maintaining and developing the Model Convention and its commentaries was fundamental to the Committee’s technical mandate. Several members highlighted the importance of avoiding duplication of work with other subcommittees to be established by the Committee. Some members called for the Committee to define clear modalities of collaboration between subcommittees, enabling cross-cutting technical work to enhance integration between different workstreams, in particular as to the digital economy, extractive industries and transfer pricing, while maintaining overall coherence in the Model Convention. One member suggested that, instead of a stand-alone structure, the Committee could group issues by theme (e.g. allocation of taxing rights, attribution of profits) and allow each relevant subcommittee to contribute directly to the Model Convention updates. The Committee subsequently agreed in a closed meeting that each subcommittee would identify links with other workstreams of the Committee in its proposed workplan and liaise with other relevant subcommittees to ensure coherence among the Committee’s outputs.17.A majority of members identified article 5 (Permanent establishment) as a high-priority area for review, citing the need to modernize the concept to reflect digital and service-based business models. Some members also proposed further exploration of the concept of significant economic presence either as a complement or an alternative to traditional permanent establishment rules and called for the alignment of permanent establishment discussions with profit attribution rules under article 7.18.Other members proposed the updating of the definitions of: (a) “interest” (article 11) to reflect modern financial instruments; (b) “royalties” (article 12) to include all forms of copyright and related rights, beyond artistic, literary and scientific works; and (c) “immovable property” (article 6) to clarify the treatment of mineral royalties essential to protecting source taxing rights.19.Members reaffirmed the importance of ensuring that any updates to the Model Convention enhanced the taxing rights of developing countries and contributed meaningfully to domestic resource mobilization and progress towards the Sustainable Development Goals. Members reflected on the relationship between the technical work of the Committee and the ongoing negotiations on the United Nations framework convention on international tax cooperation. There was general agreement that the Committee should continue to meet its independent technical mandate to produce practical guidance, with special emphasis on the needs and priorities of developing countries, while maintaining awareness of potential synergies with the intergovernmental process.20.The Committee established the Subcommittee on the Update of the United Nations Model Tax Convention, taking up the mandate proposed in the conference room paper (E/C.18/2025/CRP.22) subject to minor modifications to enhance flexibility and coordination. The Committee left it to the Subcommittee to propose priority areas for consideration, but endorsed the revision of the permanent establishment concept, income from immovable property and definitions of interest and royalties as some initial priority areas. The Committee agreed that Antoine Guici and Wanjiru Kiarie would be Co-Coordinators of the Subcommittee.C.Review and possible update of the Manual for the Negotiation of Bilateral Tax Treaties between Developed and Developing Countries21.Ms. Ojeda Alvarez of the Secretariat presented a document containing options for a proposed workstream concerning the United Nations Manual for the Negotiation of Bilateral Tax Treaties between Developed and Developing Countries (E/C.18/2025/CRP.23). She outlined the background and objectives and proposed decisions for the Committee’s consideration.22.Ms. Ojeda Alvarez recalled that the Manual, which primarily served as a practical guide for negotiators from developing countries, had been first published in 1979 and subsequently revised in 2003, 2015, 2019 and, most recently, in 2023. The 2023 edition reflected the 2021 Model Convention, incorporating article 12B on income from automated digital services and guidance on virtual negotiations. 23.Ms. Ojeda Alvarez recalled the introduction of substantial updates in the 2025 Model Convention, including three new articles (5A, 12AA and 12C) new provisions and commentaries, as well as the deletion of articles 12A and 14, which had been replaced by article 12AA. The deleted articles and commentaries on them would be accessible as an annex to the 2025 Model Convention, because of their continued relevance for interpreting and applying existing treaties.24.Consequent to these changes, the Manual now requires revision to ensure consistency with the 2025 Model Convention. Moreover, a number of additions to the 2021 Model Convention were not reflected in the 2023 Manual, including treatment of collective investment vehicles, real estate investment trusts and offshore indirect transfers. Stakeholders also identified the need for the development of practical negotiation tools, such as visual guides, decision trees and timelines.25.The Committee was invited to decide on: (a)Whether to undertake an update of the Manual during the 2025–2029 term; (b)The scope of such an update: Option A (essential update, align the Manual with the 2025 Model Convention) Option B (comprehensive update, encompassing option A and, in addition, expanded guidance on negotiation practices, visual tools and practical examples); (c)Whether to establish a dedicated subcommittee; (d)Whether to update the title of the Manual to align it with that of the 2025 Model Convention.26.There was broad support for updating the Manual and for establishing a subcommittee to undertake the work. Several members suggested engaging former members of the Committee and external experts with relevant experience to support the process. Members emphasized that maintaining alignment between the Manual and the Model Convention was integral to the Committee’s mandate and highlighted the importance of the Manual as a practical tool for developing countries, as well as for capacity-building.27.A number of members supported option B (comprehensive update), emphasizing the importance of including practical guidance on negotiation challenges, model clauses and the use of visual and hybrid negotiation tools. Others favoured option A (essential update), citing the need for a realistic approach in view of the Committee’s available time and resources.28.Several members proposed a “phased” or “prioritized” approach, beginning with option A elements and subsequently incorporating option B topics, while also addressing emerging issues, such as the potential use of artificial intelligence in treaty negotiations, recognizing its possible role in supporting comparative research and preparatory analysis. 29.In addition, several members emphasized the continued relevance of articles 12A and 14, which remained present in many existing tax treaties (hence their retention, together with their commentaries, as noted above, in an annex to the 2025 Model Convention). It was suggested that guidance on those provisions be retained in the Manual in some similar fashion, to assist negotiators dealing with such treaties (e.g. in their renegotiation).30.The Committee underlined the importance of maintaining the Manual’s usability, practicality and accessibility, including using visual tools and concise explanations of key provisions. 31.The Committee agreed that the title of the Manual should be updated to reflect alignment with that of the 2025 United Nations Model Tax Convention, while preserving explicit reference to the Manual’s particular focus on developing countries. Some members supported adopting a simplified formulation, provided that its purpose and audience remained clearly identifiable. One suggestion was made to include in the title the phrase “for Developing Countries”, aligning it with similar Committee guidance.32.The Committee decided to undertake an update of the Manual and established a subcommittee to carry out the work. Gian Sandri was elected as Coordinator, and the possibility of appointing an external Co-coordinator was noted. The Committee agreed to allow the subcommittee to prioritize the elements of options A and B and requested the Subcommittee to submit proposals for the updated title of the Manual for consideration by the Committee.D.Procedural issues for the Committee33.At the present stage, the Committee did not see the need to address procedural issues.E.Other areas for Committee consideration and guidance as part of its work programme (including determining that work programme and setting up subsidiary bodies)1.Taxation of the extractive industries 34.Olivier Munyaneza of the Secretariat presented a note by the Secretariat containing proposals for potential areas of work for the Committee on the taxation of extractive industries (E/C.18/2025/CRP.27)and recalling its previous work in this area, initiated in 2014. He outlined several issues that had not been addressed by previous memberships, and that might be of interest to the current Committee, including taxation issues related to critical minerals. He outlined the proposals in the note for two possible areas of work which could be addressed simultaneously: (a) the preparation of supplementary guidance on the valuation of critical minerals, to be completed by the thirty-seventh session (March 2028); and (b) the monitoring of developments in taxation issues related to energy transition.35.Mr. Munyaneza also highlighted the relationship between the taxation of extractive industries and other Committee workstreams, including transfer pricing and environmental taxation, as well as its relevance to Sustainable Development Goals 9, 13 and 17. He emphasized the alignment of this workstream with the Committee’s mandate and recalled the previous membership’s recommendation to continue work in this area, as well as the inputs received from stakeholders in this regard.36.Following the presentation by the secretariat, members expressed broad support for the establishment of a subcommittee on the taxation of extractive industries. They emphasized the importance of ensuring close coordination between this subcommittee and other subcommittees, in particular those on environmental taxation, transfer pricing and the United Nations Model Tax Convention.37.With respect to potential areas of work, members agreed that the subcommittee should focus on both developing guidance on the valuation of critical minerals and monitoring developments related to energy transition. Several members suggested that the subcommittee’s mandate should not be limited to valuation issues but should more broadly address tax-related aspects of critical minerals, including transfer pricing and price volatility. References were also made to publications from international organizations highlighting the importance of critical minerals and the risks that base erosion posed for developing countries. One member noted that the work related to monitoring tax-related developments in energy transition could have linkages with that of a workstream on environmental taxation.38.One observer highlighted the linkages between this topic and transfer pricing issues and proposed that, in its work, the Subcommittee consider the fiscal take from critical minerals and explore the inclusion of recommendations for countries on how to negotiate tax-related matters regarding critical mineral projects, taking into consideration their increasing geopolitical significance. A second observer encouraged any such subcommittee to examine the impact of trade-related taxes on mineral exports and imports, in particular for developing countries.39.The Co-Chair, Ms. Steinnes Sønvisen, noted the strong support for establishing a subcommittee on the taxation of extractive industries, working in coordination with other relevant subcommittees. The Subcommittee was established, and Darlingston Y. Talery was elected as Coordinator.2.Taxation issues related to the digitalized and globalized economy40.Michael Lennard of the Secretariat presented a note by the Secretariat on taxation issues related to the digitalized and globalized economy (E/C.18/2025/CRP.24). The note highlighted the work of previous memberships on this topic, the recommendations from the 2021–2025 membership on potential workstreams and the inputs received from stakeholders. It also provided recommendations for the Committee.41.The note contained three questions for the Committee to consider:(a)Which outputs to pursue during the 2025–2029 term? These could include:(i)Output A only – an initial evaluation report (for submission to the Committee at its thirty-third session in October 2026) containing an assessment of whether 2025 Model Convention provisions adequately address services permanent establishments, emerging technologies (including artificial intelligence) and remote working arrangements – possibly to be followed by further action;(ii)Output B only – practical implementation guidance on taxation of services (for completion by the time of the thirty-sixth session of the Committee in March 2028) covering treaty negotiation, domestic law implementation and administrative aspects;(iii)Both outputs – evaluation report and implementation guidance; (iv)Neither output – deciding to take no action.(b)What are the priorities for Committee guidance within selected outputs, given that the Committee cannot address all issues raised by stakeholders in the current membership? That is:(i)For output A (evaluation): should all the issues (services permanent establishments, emerging technology, remote workers) receive equal focus, or should one or two be prioritized?(ii)For output B (implementation guidance): which areas are most urgent: treaty negotiation, domestic law or administrative aspects, or are they all interconnected? Should guidance address all developing countries equally or provide differentiated guidance for different capacity levels? (c)Whether to establish a subcommittee to undertake the work and provide guidance on its composition?42.On the question of which outputs to pursue, members agreed that, while all the issues were important, some held greater significance for developing countries. For output A, it was noted that physical presence was emphasized in most treaties, but there was a need also to consider other aspects such as “virtual” permanent establishments and the concept of “significant economic presence.” It was also suggested that output A was a good way of focusing the work of this Committee on the optimal use of its available resources.43.Other members considered that working on both outputs was the best way forward. It was noted that practical guidance was very important to guide developing countries in the implementation of the provisions previously updated or introduced by the Committee. Towards that end, some members noted that treaty negotiation, domestic law or administrative aspects of the taxation of services are all interconnected and should all be addressed. 44.Other members considered that the Committee should prioritize implementation of the Model Convention provisions with regard to the services permanent establishment and to emerging technologies. Another area that was identified was tax treatment of remote workers. It was noted that the previous membership had commenced work on the taxation of remote working and had considered a possible new article 15 (4) to be included as an option in the Commentary to article 15 and that this could be further explored by the present Committee.45.Observers noted that the practical guidance as proposed for output B was such that it would give immediate practical benefit to developing countries, as it ensured that the work of the previous Committee on taxation of services could be more widely adopted. Some stated that they had started to include these provisions in their treaty negotiations, but there were challenges in reaching a common understanding. Practical guidance would assist in dealing with these challenges.46.On the issue of remote workers, it was observed that this was not a priority for most developing countries, as they were mainly “exporters” of remote workers; remote workers were hence not likely to bring revenues to these countries. Another perspective offered was that, if the practical challenge of double deduction of tax at source on employment income could be addressed, remote working could increasingly offer the prospect of relatively well-paid employment where there were barriers to economic migration to an employer’s State. It was also noted, however, that this issue gave rise to the possibility of multiple permanent establishments created by the remote workers, and it might be helpful to examine those issues. In the end, the Committee decided not to specifically address the matter in the mandate of the subcommittee at the present stage. 47.There was broad agreement from the members and observers that a subcommittee be formed, and the Committee therefore formed a subcommittee with the mandate to prepare a report addressing the treatment of services permanent establishment and assessing whether services related to emerging technologies are adequately addressed in the Model Convention. It would also develop practical implementation guidance designed to assist countries in applying the services-related provisions of the 2025 Model Convention effectively. 48.At the present stage, the acting Co-Coordinators of the Subcommittee would be Ms. Clocheret, Mr. Gbonjubola and Claudia Pimentel.3.Transfer pricing49.Ilka Ritter of the Secretariat introduced a note by the Secretariat summarizing the work of the Committee on transfer pricing, highlighting gaps, proposed outputs and links to other workstreams and the Sustainable Development Goals (E/C.18/2025/CRP.29). The Committee commenced work on transfer pricing in 2008, resulting in the United Nations Practical Manual on Transfer Pricing for Developing Countries, first published in 2009 and updated in 2017 and 2021. The Manual provides practical guidance on the application of article 9 of the Model Convention, focused on developing countries. 50.Ms. Ritter noted that the previous membership had issued six guidance notes: two of them sectoral (pharmaceuticals; agriculture), two transaction-specific (carbon offsets and credits; coronavirus disease (COVID-19) economic downturn) and two administrative (compliance assurance; dispute avoidance and resolution).51.The secretariat outlined three proposed outputs as follows, based on Subcommittee and stakeholder recommendations: Output A – a comprehensive update of the 2021 Manual, adding or revising guidance on financial transactions (cash pooling, loan-equity distinctions, country-risk adjustments), intra-group services (expanded benefit testing), intangibles (bundled intangibles), simplification measures, including safe harbours, and updating or expanding country examples;Output B – practical guidance on profit attribution to permanent establishments under article 7, covering developments such as services permanent establishments and mobile workforce, and including commentary to article 7 and a permanent establishment profit attribution report; Output C – development of sector-specific guidance for industries important to developing countries and vulnerable to transfer pricing risks, for example, on telecommunications and tourism.52.The secretariat proposed prioritizing work sought by officials from developing countries that represents opportunities for domestic resource mobilization and takes into account the current membership’s expertise. 53.For output A, the secretariat noted its feasibility within the four-year term, citing prior update experience and existing expertise. For output B, the secretariat stressed that guidance on the allocation of profits to permanent establishments would support the evolving Model Convention, strengthen resource mobilization and reduce disputes. For output C, the secretariat noted ongoing demand and feasibility. The secretariat invited the Committee to decide whether to pursue transfer pricing as a Committee workstream, which outputs to prioritize and whether to establish a subcommittee.54.Members strongly supported continuing transfer pricing guidance work and establishing a subcommittee, emphasizing its importance for resource mobilization in developing countries.55.On output A, members and observers agreed that the Manual is essential and must remain current. They emphasized the need for updated, practical guidance tailored to developing countries, focusing on capacity development, the simplification of the arm’s length principle and access to reliable comparability data. 56.On output B, several noted that profit attribution to permanent establishments is a major source of double taxation. Some members and Member State observers suggested reassessing the suitability of the arms-length principle and considering alternative approaches, including formulary or fractional apportionment. Others cautioned that the arm’s-length principle remains a global standard and should be refined, not replaced. A Member State observer proposed the inclusion of profit attribution in the workplan of the Committee, to be handled by this subcommittee or by the subcommittees on the Model Convention or on Taxation of the Digitalized and Globalized Economy.57.On output C, several speakers strongly supported continued sector-specific guidance as a practical tool for administrations and taxpayers. They identified telecommunications and tourism as high-risk, revenue-significant sectors and recommended a risk-based approach towards prioritization. Many cited limited access to reliable comparables as a key challenge and called for improved data access, sharing and affordability. Some suggested exploring a United Nations-sponsored or regional database. A Member State observer noted the growing relevance of guidance on algorithmic value creation and data-driven profitability.58.Some Member State observers highlighted the link between transfer pricing, illicit financial flows and revenue loss, calling for stronger regional cooperation and experience sharing to promote coherent regional approaches.59.Observers from the private sector and academia welcomed a multi-stakeholder approach, consistent with the practice of previous subcommittees on this topic. They stressed the importance of keeping the Manual practical and current, reflecting evolving business models, including data-driven and digital activities. They encouraged giving the Subcommittee flexibility to define its sectoral and overall scope of work, considering national contexts, available resources, expertise and linkages to other workstreams. Several echoed the need for flexibility in the Subcommittee’s scope and timeline, given available resources and coordination requirements.60.The Co-Chair, Ms. Steinnes Sønvisen, acknowledged strong support for this workstream and stated that a subcommittee on transfer pricing would be established. She stated that the mandate of the Subcommittee would include updating the 2021 Manual; developing guidance on transfer pricing in specific sectors/industries; and liaising with other relevant Subcommittees on possible work on profit attribution to permanent establishments. In addition, it was decided that the work on unilateral advance pricing arrangements (see para. 84 regarding dispute resolution and avoidance) would also form a part of the work programme of the Subcommittee. 61.The Committee agreed on Mr. Gbonjubola, Wanda Montero Cuello and Trude Steinnes Sønvisen as the acting Co-Coordinators of the Subcommittee.4.Indirect taxes62.Ilka Ritter of the Secretariat introduced (on behalf of Farid Hasnaoui) a document on indirect taxes with a primary focus on value added tax (VAT)/goods and services tax (GST) (E/C.18/2025/CRP.28). She recalled that the 2021–2025 membership had approved four guidance papers, covering: (a) an overview of VAT/GST in developing countries; (b) VAT/GST treatment of small enterprises; (c) an introduction to VAT/GST refunds; and (d) the use of new technologies to improve compliance, including e-invoicing and big data.63.Ms. Ritter noted that, while these outputs represented significant progress, that membership had suspended, as a matter of prioritization, work on several topics, namely: sector-specific treatment for tourism and construction; interaction between VAT/GST and other taxes; and VAT/GST issues related to government entities, charities and donor-funded projects. 64.She recalled that the previous membership had recommended that the above-mentioned suspended topics be reconsidered by the incoming membership owing to their relevance for developing countries. The previous membership had also identified further areas for potential work, including the VAT treatment of Islamic financial instruments, the regressivity of VAT systems, VAT and the financial sector, VAT in the digital economy, cross-border VAT/GST dispute resolution, taxation of digital platforms and the use of electronic certificates for VAT/GST refunds.65.She added that inputs from stakeholders suggested related topics: examining the complementarity between consumption taxes and corporate income tax; and practical guidance on managing VAT/GST and customs procedures in special economic zones to prevent fraud, leakage and double non-taxation.66.Ms. Ritter presented three options for the Committee’s consideration regarding its future work in this area:Option A – a comprehensive, phased workstream addressing all major gaps through two phases, to be consolidated into a United Nations handbook series on VAT/GST:Phase 1 (years 1–3): prevention and resolution of cross-border VAT/GST disputes; sector-specific treatment (initially construction); and VAT/GST in the digital economy, focusing on practical implementation and taxation of digital platforms;Phase 2 (years 3–3.5): interaction between VAT/GST and other taxes, including corporate income tax; and VAT/GST treatment for government entities, charities and donor-funded projects;Option B – focus on phase 1 topics only and defer phase 2 for future consideration;Option C – focus exclusively on the previously suspended topics.67.Ms. Ritter emphasized the need for close coordination with related workstreams. She underlined that the proposed outputs on indirect taxes would contribute to the Sustainable Development Goals – especially Goal 17 – by providing practical tools to enhance domestic resource mobilization and by supporting the financing for development agenda and the follow-up to the Sevilla Commitment.68.Several members expressed support for option A, recognizing its comprehensive and phased approach, while others considered option B more manageable in view of available resources.69.Members identified other areas for consideration related to VAT/GST, mainly fraud, regressivity, tourism, cross-border transactions and artificial intelligence. Some highlighted the importance of developing practical guidance on the taxation of digital platforms. 70.Members and observers emphasized the central importance of VAT/GST in domestic resource mobilization, where it constitutes a major share of total tax revenues. Broad support was expressed for establishing a dedicated subcommittee to build on the achievements of the previous work. Many interventions highlighted the importance of maintaining flexibility and recommended that the subcommittee identify priorities and determine sequencing based on member expertise and available resources.71.The Committee approved the establishment of a subcommittee on indirect taxes with primary focus on VAT/GST, with a mandate to develop further draft guidance on policy and administrative issues in the design and implementation of VAT/GST systems. It will give particular attention to the priorities and challenges of developing countries and address gaps in existing international guidance. 72.The Committee elected Hajara Batamuliza, Ms. Namuyemba-Sikombe, and Hector Thompson as Co-Coordinators of the Subcommittee.5.Tax administration and artificial intelligence73.Mr. Lennard of the Secretariat presented a paper on tax-related artificial intelligence issues (E/C.18/2025/CRP.25). He noted that the focus in the paper was on the use of artificial intelligence in tax administrations – in particular in developing countries – rather than on the allocation of taxing rights over artificial intelligence-based services. The latter, he noted, could be considered by the Subcommittee on the Taxation of the Digitalized and Globalized Economy.74.The secretariat reviewed recent work undertaken by the Committee, as well as by other relevant international and regional organizations, and identified a gap in practical, implementation-focused guidance tailored to developing countries.75.The secretariat suggested that possible outputs under this workstream could cover:(a)The opportunities (e.g. fraud detection, risk assessment, taxpayer services and dispute management) and risks (e.g. tax avoidance or evasion, data privacy threats and employment and ethical challenges) associated with the use of artificial intelligence in tax administrations;(b)Use cases adapted to the context of developing countries; (c)“Enabling environment” and governance issues, including potential new regulatory frameworks.76.The secretariat also recognized the relationship between this workstream and others – such as those on transfer pricing, indirect taxation and dispute avoidance and resolution – and its relevance to Sustainable Development Goals 16 (effective, accountable institutions) and 17 (enhancing domestic resource mobilization and capacity support for developing countries).77.The secretariat presented two possible options. The first would entail developing a comprehensive practical guide on artificial intelligence for tax administrations over a period of three to three and a half years. The second option would focus on high-priority issues over a two-year period, with the possibility of extending the work thereafter.78.Members expressed broad support for establishing a subcommittee dedicated to artificial intelligence and tax administration with a special focus on the needs and priorities of developing countries, emphasizing that artificial intelligence is already being adopted by some national tax authorities and that United Nations guidance could assist developing countries in implementation, capacity-building and risk mitigation. Members supported the secretariat’s proposal that the work of the Subcommittee would remain primarily focused on the administrative issues.79.Members endorsed the phased approach presented, focusing initially on high-priority issues, in view of the rapid pace of technological change and the urgency of providing early guidance to developing countries. Some members suggested that issues such as the infrastructure costs associated with implementing artificial intelligence tools in tax administrations, the risk of gender bias and the importance of maintaining human oversight should also be taken into consideration. Members proposed that any subcommittee should operate through a multidisciplinary, multi-stakeholder approach involving artificial intelligence experts, academia and international and regional organizations to ensure relevance, inclusivity and technical robustness.80.Member State and other observers reiterated the importance of collaboration with ongoing initiatives and of mapping existing work. They suggested that the Committee address issues related to data confidentiality and ethical considerations in the use of artificial intelligence, taxpayers’ rights, capacity-building for tax administration staff and the potential of artificial intelligence to reduce litigation risks and improve dispute resolution mechanisms, inter alia.81.The Co-Chair, Mr. Gbonjubola, noted the decision of the Committee to establish a subcommittee on tax administration and artificial intelligence, mandated to prepare a draft practical guide to implementing artificial intelligence for tax administrations, with a focus on high-priority issues. He also stressed that this subcommittee would draw on the work of the previous membership on the digitalization of tax administration to ensure continuity, while emphasizing that artificial intelligence represents a new and distinct frontier requiring focused attention.82.The Committee elected Jeneba Jackline Bangura as Coordinator of the Subcommittee. Alexander Smirnov was later added as a Co-Coordinator.6.Dispute avoidance and resolution83.Mr. Lennard of the Secretariat presented (on behalf of Josephine Muchiri) a note by the Secretariat on dispute avoidance and resolution (E/C.18/2025/CRP.26). The note highlighted the work that previous memberships had done on this topic, recommendations from the 2021–2025 membership on potential workstreams and the inputs received from stakeholders. It also outlined recommendations for the Committee to consider.84.The note contained proposals, as a potential output, on supplementary guidance on unilateral advance pricing arrangements addressing implementation challenges and an ongoing activity to monitor dispute avoidance and resolution across various forums, as well as feedback from capacity development activities, in order to identify whether emerging issues or experiences in country implementation reveal new gaps where practical Committee guidance for developing countries would be warranted. The note also acknowledged the ongoing work in the United Nations intergovernmental process towards a United Nations Framework Convention on International Tax Cooperation, where dispute prevention and resolution is the subject of one of the early protocols being negotiated.85.Three options were presented in the note: A.Monitoring activity only; B.Output and monitoring activity; C.No action, with no workstream established in this area. 86.The following questions were put forward for Committee:(a)Should the Committee establish this workstream?(b)Which option does it prefer?(c)Should a subcommittee be established, and what guidance on its composition should be provided?87.Members noted that the work on dispute avoidance and resolution was a critical factor when investors considered where to invest, contributing to domestic revenue mobilization for developing countries. Option B was highlighted as a good way forward on this issue. It was noted that there were many benefits to a unilateral advance pricing arrangement programme because many developing countries had narrow treaty networks and even where they had treaties in place, the corresponding treaty partners were not always interested in a bilateral advance pricing arrangement. 88.A member suggested that this work could be carried out within the Subcommittee on Transfer Pricing as a fourth option, since most relevant disputes would be transfer pricing-related and the previous Subcommittee on Transfer Pricing had developed guidance on bilateral advance pricing arrangements which could assist the proposed work. 89.Some observers noted that the Subcommittee on Transfer Pricing seemed to have a full agenda, and it might be better to have a dedicated subcommittee dealing with the work on unilateral advance pricing arrangements. 90.A member also highlighted the risk of the monitoring function falling by the wayside if there was no dedicated subcommittee on dispute avoidance and resolution. 91.There was broad agreement from the members and observers that the work on unilateral advance pricing arrangements be carried out, and the Committee mandated the Subcommittee on Transfer Pricing to do so. The Committee also formed an ad hoc group on dispute avoidance and resolution, mandated to monitor developments in dispute avoidance and resolution, as well as to monitor feedback from capacity development activities. It was decided that Mr. Sandri would be Coordinator of this work.7.Matters not covered in conference room papers (including environmental taxes, wealth and solidarity taxes, tax and human rights, tax and gender)92.The Co-Chair, Ms. Steinnes Sønvisen, outlined potential additional work areas not separately covered by conference papers, as summarized in note E/C.18/2025/CRP.30. The discussions are reflected in paragraphs 93 to 99 below. (a)Environmental taxation93.On environmental taxation, one member noted that environmental taxes can provide additional revenues while encouraging a cleaner environment. The member added that developing countries, facing disproportionate climate impacts and large youth populations, would benefit from additional practical guidance. Some members supported establishing a subcommittee on this topic, commending the Committee’s past work in this area and stressing the need to address practical issues. Members affirmed that environmental taxes support domestic resource mobilization and international tax cooperation and are crucial for the Sustainable Development Goals and climate action. In line with the recommendations made by the previous Subcommittee, they proposed extending work beyond carbon to other environmental levies, with safeguards for low-income households and sector-specific guidance for aviation and maritime transport. Some members proposed specific guidance on environmental taxation in the extractive industries. 94.An observer representing the aviation industry expressed the view that taxes on air transport have not reliably led to measurable CO2 emissions reductions and may hinder affordability and decarbonization investment, and recommended reliance on the Carbon Offsetting and Reduction Scheme for International Aviation, developed by the International Civil Aviation Organization. Another observer emphasized the importance of aligning tax policy and administration with evolving global carbon and plastic initiatives, noting that most environmental taxes are indirect and require business input for practical, enforceable design.95.The Co-Chair acknowledged broad support for an environmental taxation workstream and the establishment of a subcommittee. The Committee elected Giedrė Lideikytė Huber as the Coordinator of this subcommittee. (b)Wealth taxation 96.The Co-Chair recalled the previous membership’s work on the United Nations Handbook on Wealth and Solidarity Taxes, noting the Subcommittee’s recommendation to continue work on wealth taxation. One member proposed focusing on the taxation of high-net-worth individuals, noting that tax administrations’ limited information on the wealth of high-net-worth individuals in developing countries undermines their tax base and that stronger taxation would advance equity and revenue mobilization. 97.Members commended the progress achieved and proposed additional work on high-net-worth individuals to identify base erosion risks, develop ideas for progressive taxation approaches, enhance information exchange and cooperation and strengthen administrative capacity. Members highlighted challenges with regard to capital mobility and emphasized the need for cross-jurisdictional coordination, for developing-country audit capacity and for alignment with international standards.98.An observer noted that article 22 of the Model Convention provides limited source-based taxing rights over wealth and suggested exploring options to improve treaty compatibility for countries introducing or expanding net wealth taxes. Another observer urged focusing on tax avoidance by high-net-worth individuals, while also taking account of risks of double taxation and reduced investment and job creation from excessive taxation.99.The Co-Chair acknowledged the support for this workstream and for the establishment of a subcommittee on wealth taxation. The Committee elected Jorge Ferreras Gutiérrez as the Coordinator of this Subcommittee.(c)Taxation of cryptoassets100.Members recognized the efforts made by the previous Committee membership and its Ad Hoc Group on the Taxation of Cryptoassets in producing the toolkit on this topic and called for more practical guidance for developing countries amid rapid cryptoasset developments. They highlighted links to artificial intelligence and related tax risks. At the present stage, however, it was decided to defer decision on establishing a workstream in this area. (d)Digitalization and other opportunities to improve tax administration101.The Co-Chair recalled work from the previous membership on the forthcoming United Nations guide to digitalization for revenue authorities. Members supported continuing work on digitalization within the Subcommittee on Tax Administration and Artificial Intelligence, stressing flexibility in scope.(e)Tax transparency 102.The Co-Chair recalled work from the previous membership on the forthcoming guide to increasing tax transparency. Members and observers emphasized strong links between tax transparency and workstreams on high-net-worth individuals, cryptoassets and digitalization. Members noted the need to improve developing countries’ access to information exchange, despite time and resource constraints. It was agreed that tax transparency would be discussed within the relevant workstreams.(f)Health taxes103.The Co-Chair recalled the prior membership’s work in producing the United Nations Handbook on Health Taxes for Developing Countries. Members recommended focusing on practical implementation, prioritizing capacity-building, translation and dissemination of the Handbook.(g)Tax, trade and investment agreements 104.The Co-Chair noted that this area of work did not need to be further pursued by the current membership. Meanwhile, dissemination of the guidance produced by the previous membership would continue.(h)Other topics105.The Co-Chair introduced additional topics that had been raised, including taxation and human rights, gender and taxation, taxation of the informal sector and taxation of betting and gambling.106.Members and observers strongly supported the establishment of a workstream and subcommittee on taxation and gender, noting their alignment with Sustainable Development Goal 5 in particular, and stressing that achieving gender equality in taxation requires both policy and administrative action. Proposed work included developing a gender-impact-assessment framework, promoting women’s leadership in tax administration and reviewing guidance issued by the United Nations Tax Committee through a gender lens. Several members and observers highlighted the link between gender and the informal sector, especially in low-income countries, and members and observers agreed that emphasis on the informal sector would help ensure a practical focus in the Subcommittee’s work. Any guidance on gender and taxation can strengthen resource mobilization and equity. The Committee formed the subcommittee and elected Ms. Bangura and Ms. Namuyemba-Sikombe as the CoCoordinators.8.Secretariat presentation on capacity-building107.The Chief of the International Tax and Development Cooperation Branch in the Financing for Sustainable Development Office, Caroline Lombardo, delivered a presentation on how the work of the Committee supports capacity-building efforts, highlighting the integrated approach of the United Nations to its tax work, enabled by the Department of Economic and Social Affairs multi-donor project to strengthen effective support for the work of the United Nations Tax Committee and related capacity development activities.108.Ms. Lombardo highlighted the Committee’s mandate to make recommendations on capacity-building. These recommendations are conveyed through the Committee’s discussions, reports and guidance products. This guidance underpins capacity-building at all levels. A core function of the secretariat, she noted, is also to bring the feedback from these activities to the Committee, ensuring that its guidance remains relevant to country priorities. 109.Ms. Lombardo emphasized the importance of a continuous feedback loop between norm- and policy-shaping and capacity-building. The United Nations tax work integrates policy development with practical implementation: while the Committee produces technical guidance, the United Nations and its partners – facilitated by the secretariat – deliver workshops, training and technical assistance. Developing countries actively contribute by providing feedback on areas requiring further work, ensuring quality, relevance and responsiveness to diverse contexts.110.Ms. Lombardo outlined three dimensions of the multi-donor project launched in 2019 to support the Committee and related capacity development work. The project enables delivery by the Committee of practical guidance tailored to developing countries’ priorities, strengthens countries’ tax systems through the lens of the Sustainable Development Goals and promotes taxation as a policy lever for sustainable development. It has also supported developing countries’ greater participation in international tax forums and enhanced their ability to contribute to global discussions. 111.In that context, Ms. Lombardo also noted how the project would allow for printed and translated versions of all the approved outputs of the 2021–2025 Committee membership. 112.The Chief of the Capacity Development Unit, Emily Muyaa, then gave a briefing to the Committee on recent capacity-building activities, highlighting workshops on tax treaties and transfer pricing responding to country demand. These included a preparatory workshop on tax treaty negotiation held in Seoul in June 2025 and the eighth Joint United Nations-Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development practical workshop on tax treaty negotiation in Vienna, held in July, both with strong participation by small island developing States. Regional workshops on key transfer pricing topics were held in Africa, in Addis Ababa, in July and in Asia-Pacific, in Incheon, in August. 113.Ms. Muyaa gave a briefing on the latest iterations of the Community of Practice workshops, launched in 2022, with sessions in August and September covering key services-related provisions of the 2025 Model Convention, namely, articles (12AA (fees for services); 5A (contractors in the extractive industries); 12C (insurance premiums); and 1(3) (subject-to-tax rule). In October, a virtual workshop coorganized with the South Centre and the Brazilian presidency of the Regional Platform for Tax Cooperation in Latin America and the Caribbean, had supported the dissemination of the United Nations Handbook on Wealth and Solidarity Taxes. She also recalled the accessibility of the Department of Economic and Social Affairs online tax course and the ongoing collaboration under the Platform for Collaboration on Tax. 114.The secretariat concluded by thanking donors – Norway, Denmark, the European Commission, India and Singapore – and Committee members and other collaborating stakeholders for their support. 115.Participants welcomed the secretariat’s presentation and emphasized the importance of disseminating the Committee’s guidance products, while continuing to develop practical guidance that responded to developing country priorities and diverse, real-world contexts.9.Work programme of the Committee and United Nations Framework Convention developments116.The Committee discussed its technical work programme in the light of the ongoing negotiations of the United Nations framework convention on international tax cooperation, including its first protocols.117.The Co-Chair, Mr. Gbonjubola, explained that some members had previously enquired about how the work of the Committee would interact with that of the intergovernmental negotiating committee. He emphasized the distinct, yet complementary, roles of the framework convention process, focused on political and legal architecture, and the Committee’s expert process, focused on practical, technical guidance.118.The Co-Chair noted, by way of example, the early protocols under negotiation in the intergovernmental process – on dispute prevention and resolution and on cross-border services. Once adopted, countries could seek practical guidance from the United Nations Tax Committee to support their implementation. 119.The secretariat provided an update on the Intergovernmental Negotiating Committee developments and timeline. The adoption of the Framework Convention is expected by 2027, while the Committee’s current mandate extends through 2029 – creating a valuable two-year overlap for mutual reinforcement between the two processes. Speakers expressed broad agreement that the two processes should be complementary but independent. 120.The Co-Chair concluded that the Committee would remain focused on developing practical instruments and guidance – some of which could later inform or operationalize the Convention’s provisions. He reaffirmed that the work of the United Nations Tax Committee should complement, not duplicate, the Convention negotiations and that both processes should evolve “side by side”.10.Agreement on Subcommittee mandates121.The subcommittee and ad hoc group mandates as agreed by the Committee are made available on the Committee website: https://financing.desa.un.org/what-we-do/ECOSOC/tax-committee/tax-committee-home. Agenda item 6: Provisional agenda for the thirty-second session122.The secretariat presented the provisional agenda for the thirty-second session for approval by the Economic and Social Council, as set out in chapter I of the present report. The Committee was informed that the next session would be held in New York from 23 to 26 March 2026, to be followed by the special meeting of the Economic and Social Council on international cooperation in tax matters on 27 March 2026.Agenda item 7: Arrangements for adopting the report of the Committee on its thirty-first session123.The Secretary noted that arrangements for the report on the thirty-first session would, as usual, be as provided for in the Committee document entitled “Practices and Working Methods for the Committee of Experts on International Cooperation in Tax Matters.” Closing remarks124.The Co-Chairs thanked Committee members and observers for their in-person participation in the session, acknowledging the valuable contribution of observers and of those countries financially supporting the work of the Committee. They also thanked the secretariat and those providing interpretation and conference services in Geneva. They noted that Michael Lennard would be retiring in January 2026 and thanked him for his service over 19 years and 30 sessions. The secretariat thanked all those participating, in particular the Co-Chairs, for their skilled handling of the session, and the Bureau. After a final closed meeting, the session was formally concluded. DOCVARIABLE "jobn" \* MERGEFORMAT 25-19909 (E) 060126 DOCVARIABLE "Barcode" \* MERGEFORMAT *2519909*2669540304800
